In relation to the criterion of acceptance and recognition, ILC clarified that both bring a distinct 

perspective from the acceptance and recognition required for customary international law. In this case, 

the two elements are required to answer the question of whether the rule has a peremptory character, as 

to say from which no derogation is permitted, except for a subsequent norm having the same character. 

According to the ILC, determination of such elements involves the “weighing and assessment of evidence”59, 

and it is not sufficient merely to assert such acceptance and recognition. Even if the threshold required 

for peremptory status is very high, it does not require universal consensus, as the relevant practice shall be 

representative of a vast majority of States and qualitatively significant, as such a vast majority must 

represent regions, legal systems and cultures60. The practice of other actors may be relevant in providing 

context and for assessing acceptance and recognition by the international community of States as a 

whole61, but these practices cannot, by themselves, form part of such a process62. Among the practices 

may be included any act or document of the State capable of reflecting its view in respect of the norm63. 

It should be noted that, in this framework, decisions of international and national courts represent 

subsidiary means for determining the identification of a peremptory norm, together with the works of 

expert bodies established by States or international organizations and the teachings of the most highly 

qualified publicists of the various nations. Throughout time, several international tribunals have relied on 

decisions adopted by other courts. Significantly, the case law of the IACtHR has also been recalled by 

the ILC as the Special Tribunal for Lebanon in the El Sayed case, which stated that the right to access 

justice acquired the status of a ius cogens, recalling, inter alia, the decision of the judges of San José in the 

case of Goiburú et al. v. Paraguay64.

5. On the IACtHR’s Methodology to Determine the Existence of a Peremptory Rule 

In the Advisory Opinion under analysis, the Court linked the peremptoriness of the prohibition to 

produce massive and irreversible damage to the environment, and to the climate system as part of the 

environment, primarily to the principle of effectiveness, summed with considerations of dependence, 

necessity, universality of the underlying values and the absence of conflict with current law. In a 

complementary way, such a norm contributes to compliance with existing obligations, as it requires all 

States to cooperate in ceasing conducts that violate this prohibition65. 

Drawing from the abovementioned work of the ILC, at first sight, it seems that the Court refers to 

additional elements from what has been recognised by the ILC as the elements for identifying ius cogens. 

References to effectiveness, dependence, necessity, and the absence of conflict with current law seem to 

go beyond the requirement previously analysed. This perspective leads us to try to answer the following 

question: does the identification of ius cogens made by the IACtHR satisfy the criteria listed by the ILC? 

According to the International Law Commission, the identification requires a two-step approach in which 

it is necessary to establish that the norm is of general international law and that non-derogation is 

admissible. The existence of such criteria must be demonstrated and should not be assumed they exist, 

nor is it sufficient to affirm the importance or the role of a norm, in order to show the peremptory 

character of that norm. In this sense, the two-step approach requested by the ILC appears to be a 

stringent standard of proof that many Courts have not consistently applied in their decision-making 

process66. Undoubtedly, the IACtHR is not bound by the ILC Draft Conclusions and does not have to 

prove adherence to the criteria set out by the Commission67.  

Looking at the previous relevant jurisprudence of the Court, in the Advisory Opinion No. 20 on The 

Obligations in Matter of Human Rights of a State that Has Denounced the American Convention on Human Rights and 

the Charter of the Organization of the American States, the Court described ius cogens as the “legal expression of the 

                                                             
Court”. STL, Prosecutor v. El Sayed, Order Assigning Matter to Pre-Trial Judge, CH/PRES/2010/01, 15 April 2010, para

international community as a whole, based on universal and superior values, which embodies basic standards that guarantee

norms “protect fundamental rights and universal values without which society would not prosper”69. 

So perceived, the identification of ius cogens seems to adopt a deductive approach, as it is assumed that a 

norm is peremptory whether it is perceived as necessary to protect the fundamental values of the 

international community as a whole70, or intrinsically superior to other norms, even without considering 

the attitude of States71. 

In addition, the IACtHR has made a consistent use of the concept of ius cogens, particularly regarding: 

equality and non-discrimination; torture, cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment; enforced disappearance 

of persons; slavery and other similar practices; non-refoulement; serious, massive or systematic human rights 

violations; crimes against humanity and the associated obligation to prosecute, investigate and punish 

those crimes72. 

In many circumstances, the identification of a peremptory norm has been made using a semi-deductive 

approach, by affirming the universal and fundamental value surrounded by the specific rule or principle 

at stake73, while in others, the Court employed a functional approach, asserting the dependence of the 

particular norm on others considered to be of ius cogens, as necessary to ensure the effective 

implementation of, or derivation from, them. 

In our view, with specific reference to the case under discussion, the Court used a combination of both 

approaches.  

On the one side, the previously mentioned semi-deductive approach makes the Court rely on a more 

“natural law” perspective and moral considerations74, elevating the duty not to cause irreversible damage

fundamental human rights that are already protected under international law by peremptory provisions”78. In this line, 

such an obligation must be peremptory, as it is a norm necessary for the protection of other norms of ius 

cogens. 

Therefore, the deductive approach has been balanced by a functional approach that does not disregard 

the work of the ILC, in the sense that it has relied on already existing peremptory rules and principles,

Finally, a consideration should be devoted to highlight the conceptual contradiction between the 

affirmation of the rights of Nature, in the strict sense, and the peremptory norm identified, since the 

perspective of necessity and effectiveness of human rights, posed by the Courts at the basis of the 

identification, embodies a mainly anthropocentric approach, connected to that idea of “human 

environment” affirmed by the 1972 Stockholm Declaration on Human Environment79. Perhaps the 

Court could have relied more on the concept of Nature in this specific aspect to ensure greater 

consistency between anthropocentric and ecological approaches. 

 

6. A Regional Approach to Identifying Peremptory Norms of International Law 

The statement made by the Court on the peremptory status of the duty not to cause irreversible damage 

to the environment and the climate system leads us to consider some additional aspects regarding another 

long-standing issue in international law, namely the possible existence of a regional peremptory norm. 

With this term, similarly to regional customs, legal doctrine has attempted to describe a hypothetical 

peremptory norm that develops in a specific geographical area. 

Several authors, including the ILC, have questioned the possible existence of a regional peremptory norm, 

raising doubts regarding, in particular, the conflict between the universal nature of peremptory norms 

and the regional nature of such a presumed norm, the definition of “region”, and the relationship between 

regional ius cogens and treaties concluded at the universal level. These doubts are, moreover, accompanied 

by a certain reticence on the part of States to consider the possible existence of peremptory norms, as 

they are seen as a further limitation on the scope for State action at the international level. 

The ILC, however, has adopted a more pragmatic approach, excluding the topic from its work on 

peremptory rules, as it is an issue that does not align with the universalistic aspiration that characterises 

the subsidiary body’s activities. 

However, it is a fact that the Inter-American Commission on Human Rights, as early as 1987, had stated 

that the norm prohibiting the execution of minors is recognised as having the status of ius cogens by the 

member states of the Organisation of American States80.

It has been also argued that the possibility of regional ius cogens, beyond the provisions of the Vienna 

Convention on the Law of Treaties, which, in the opinion of some authors, referred to “universal” ius 

cogens and that there is, at least in abstracto, the possibility that a specific community recognize a peremptory 

norm as pertaining to a specific regional area81.  

Doubts, however, remain. The practice of both the Commission and the IACtHR has not further 

supported this statement. Therefore, when the latter have invoked the concept of ius cogens, judges have 

always emphasised the universality of the values underlying the norm, thereby transcending, at least 

formally, the idea of a regional peremptory norm. This has been also the case of the duty to protect the 

environment from irreversible damage, as the IACtHR never mentioned a possible regional character of 

the norm, preferring instead to mention the universal character of the duty. Even in the abovementioned 

case, the statement of the Inter-American Commission on the prohibition of execution of minors should 

be read as reflecting the recognition by American States of the peremptory nature of the norm, instead 

of an identification of a universal norm of ius cogens, which requires an acceptance and recognition by the 

international community as a whole. 

In addition, the general nature of the peremptory norm represents a logical obstacle that is difficult to 

overcome until the existence of a regional mandatory norm is affirmed. Both the ILC and the ICJ 

affirmed that the meaning of “norms of general international law”, in the context of ius cogens, “refers to the 

scope of applicability of the norm in question”, and that norms “must have equal force for all members of the international 

community”82. 

The prohibition of irreversible pollution of the environment and the climate system cannot, therefore, 

logically be considered a regional peremptory norm, as it expresses fundamental values of universal 

interest to the international community of States as a whole. This conclusion, however, leads us to 

consider further the methodology used by the IACtHR to identify the peremptory norm since, as we 

have seen, the logical-legal reasoning employed by the Court partially departs from the two-step approach 

determined by the ILC. 

The use of the semi-deductive method described above, combined with a functional perspective based 

on the necessity of the norm, outlines a regional approach to identifying peremptory norms, aimed at 

ensuring the effectiveness of human rights protection. This approach, as emphasised by some scholars,

has led to a certain vivacity on the part of the Court in identifying new peremptory norms and thus to

At this point, the question arises as to what value to give to these rulings of the regional courts, and in 

particular of the IACtHR, with respect to these “new” norms of ius cogens. A violation of a peremptory 

norm of international law has significant legal consequences. When a treaty conflicts with such a norm, 

it is rendered void, releasing the Parties from any obligations to perform under it. States are then required 

to eliminate the consequences of actions taken based on the invalid treaty and to align their relations with 

the overriding norm. Furthermore, peremptory norms create obligations owed to the international 

community as a whole, allowing any state to invoke the responsibility of another for breaches of these 

norms. Serious violations entail further obligations, where States must cooperate to remedy the breach 

and cannot recognise the legality of the resulting situation or provide assistance to maintain it84. 

However, the Court did not explore such consequences in the case of the prohibition at stake, reminding 

States that they only have to cooperate to end the conduct that violates the norm. This limited effect, 

recognised by the Court, appears to be linked to a more “promotional” effect of ius cogens, particularly in 

the context of a regional legal system, in the sense that it serves as a precedent to further the evolution 

of international law, if confirmed by subsequent practice85. The envisaged perspective aligns with the 

predominant “natural law” approach followed by the Court, which holds that the protection of the 

environment and the climate system deserves to be recognised as a fundamental value. Still, all the effects 

and consequences associated with traditional peremptory rules and principles are not explicitly tied to 

such a norm. This reluctant attitude of the judges in recognising the full potential of a peremptory norm 

is evident in the use of verbs, whereby the Court affirms that States “should” cooperate and does not use

adopted in 1976 during the first reading of the topic “State responsibility”, the ILC had referred to 

obligations “of essential importance for the safeguarding and preservation of the human environment, such as those 

prohibiting massive pollution of the atmosphere or of the seas” as fundamental for the international community of 

States as a whole89. 

 

7. Closing Remarks 

In recent years, the discourse surrounding environmental protection and human rights has increasingly 

emphasised the need to establish robust legal frameworks to address the escalating climate crisis. The 

concept of ius cogens has emerged as a fundamental concept guiding this discourse. While some may argue 

that the prohibition of causing irreversible damage to the environment is a universally accepted

peremptory norm, it is imperative to consider the regional context through which the Inter-American 

Court of Human Rights has approached the identification and affirmation of such norms.  

In its Advisory Opinion on human rights obligations amidst the climate emergency, the IACtHR 

reframed the dialogue not only to recognise the significance of environmental protection but also to 

emphasise the unique regional dimensions that influence the interpretation of peremptory norms. The 

Court’s examination of climate-related human rights issues signalled a nuanced understanding that 

integrates regional characteristics – cultural, socio-economic, and environmental – into the broader 

framework of international law90. 

The IACtHR has always specifically crafted its rulings to reflect the context of the Americas, a region 

uniquely affected by various environmental challenges, including deforestation, biodiversity loss, and 

vulnerability to the impacts of climate change91. The interplay between environmental issues and human 

rights within this regional context enabled the Court to assert the urgency of addressing these concerns 

through a tailored approach. Thus, rather than merely transposing universal principles, the Court adeptly 

preliminarily recognised the particularities of the region that necessitated complementary legal 

considerations. 

Moreover, the IACtHR’s methodology in identifying peremptory norms reflects a broader trend toward 

regional interpretations of universal values recognized by international law92. While traditional 

frameworks of ius cogens often emphasise universally binding norms, the Court’s approach underlines the 

importance of other factors influencing legal recognition, such as effectiveness and necessity. This shift 

invites a more dynamic understanding of the evolution of international law, influenced also by normative 

trends that emerged at the regional level. 

By situating the prohibition of causing irreversible damage to the environment within the regional 

context, the IACtHR fosters a critical dialogue among international and national courts invested with 

disputes concerning the interpretation and implementation of climate obligations, as well as regarding 

the legitimacy and efficacy of such norms, promoting an evolutionary interpretation of international 

norms93.

Furthermore, the ICJ observed that all States have a common interest in the protection of global 

environmental commons and, consequently, States’ obligations related to the protection of the climate 

system, particularly the duty to prevent significant transboundary harm, are obligations erga omnes97. 

In line with this statement, we cannot assume an equivalence between ius cogens and obligations erga omnes, 

as they are conceptually different notions. Even if all peremptory norms of international law have an erga 

omnes effect, it is clear that not all the latter obligations arise from ius cogens. The ILC, in its comment to 

Draft Conclusion 17, recalled that some rules regarding common spaces may produce erga omnes 

obligations “independent of whether they have peremptory status”98.

Swinging Peripheries and Centres in the EU: 

Comparative Takes * 

 
 

by Sabrina Ragone 

Associate Professor of Comparative Law 

University of Bologna 

 

 

 

Abstract [En]: This paper examines the centre–periphery cleavage in the context of the European Union, 
assessing unequal relationships and developmental dynamics among its Member States in order to highlight 
interconnections, power imbalances, and economic disparities. The centre–periphery model, together with 
comparative law and legal geography methodologies, serves as a methodological framework to illustrate the 
configuration and reconfiguration of EU Member States in times of crisis: the economic and pandemic crises, the 
refugee crisis, and the current value-based crisis. 
 
Title: Periferie e centri oscillanti nell'UE: approcci comparativi 
Abstract [It]: Il saggio affronta la tematica de cleavage centro-periferia nel contesto dell’Unione Europea, 
evidenziando le dinamiche evolutive e le divergenze tra i diversi Stati Membri, con riferimento agli squilibri di 
potere e alle disparità giuridico-economiche. Il modello “centro-periferia”, insieme allo studio comparato del diritto 
e alla Legal Geography forniscono efficaci strumenti metodologici per descrivere la configurazione/riconfigurazione 
del centro e della periferia dell’Unione, specie in tempi di crisi: dalla crisi economica alla pandemia, dalla crisi 
migratoria all’attuale conflitto sull’interpretazione dei valori. 
 
Keywords: Centre-periphery approach, European Union, economic crisis, refugee crisis, European values, Article 
2 TEU, comparative law, legal geography 
Parole chiave: Approccio centro-periferia; Unione Europea, crisi economica, crisi dei rifugiati, Valori europei; 
Articolo 2 TUE; diritto comparato, geografia giuridica 
 
Summary: 1. The Centre-Periphery Cleavage as a Key Analytical Framework. 2. The Formation of Centres and 
Peripheries within the EU integration process. 3. The Economic Cleavage: from the Financial Crisis to the 
COVID-19 Pandemic. 4. The Solidarity Cleavage in Refugee and Migration Management. 5. The Value-based 
Cleavage. 6. To Conclude: Towards Even More Swinging Cleavages? 
 

1. The Centre-Periphery Cleavage as a Key Analytical Framework  

This paper explores the centre–periphery cleavage in the context of the European Union (EU), analysing 

unequal relationships and developmental dynamics among Member States in order to reveal 

interconnections, power imbalances, and economic disparities within the Union. The traditional approach 

includes in the core (or centre) the wealthier, more economically developed, and politically influential

countries like Germany, France, or some Nordic countries with stable economies, high levels of 

industrialization, and influence in the decision-making process. Peripheral countries are typically less 

economically developed, often possessing weaker political influence or a lower degree of integration into 

EU structures such as the Eurozone (though not necessarily). Geographical location also plays a role. 

For instance, in Southern Europe, Greece and Portugal have often been labelled as peripheral; in the 

East, Poland, Hungary, Romania, or Bulgaria, among others, are usually included in this category. These 

countries may rely more heavily on agriculture or services, exhibit higher unemployment rates, and thus 

depend largely on cohesion funds, being net recipients of the EU budget. Factors such as the date of 

accession are also taken into account. 

In sum, the centre–periphery approach serves as a valuable analytical tool to investigate the cleavages 

among EU Member States and to analyse the process of “centre–peripheries formation” within the 

Union and its legal order1. These cleavages have mostly been explored by political scientists, economists, 

and sociologists who have dedicated a significant body of writing to the dividing lines separating the 

founding Member States (the core/centre) from the newer Eastern and Central European Member States; 

or, with regard to the 2004 enlargement, the fifteen Member States of 1995 (Western liberal and 

economically advanced democracies) from the peripheral candidates (post-communist, politically and 

socially unstable countries). More recently, scholars engaging in legal geography2, have read the EU 

integration process in “spatial terms”, that is, to construct legal categories to make sense of the political, 

institutional, and distributive implications of EU law, and to provide a comprehensive picture of the 

multiple cleavages that exist between the “centre” and the “peripher(ies)” in the European Union3.  

The classification of a country at the “centre/core” or the “periphery” of the EU integration process 

depends on multiple factors and, primarily, on the policy area and the EU law measures under scrutiny 

(economic and fiscal law, migration and refugee law, emergency law during COVID-19, etc.), and the 

timespan considered. In this respect, Italy provides a good example. As one of the six founding States, it 

displays a low level of differentiation in terms of EU integration; however, in economic terms and 

regarding its political-economy response to the financial crisis, Italy can be associated with other countries 

in the Southern “periphery” of the EU4. It follows that the classification of Italy  – and many other

Departing from traditional approaches, this paper argues that comparative methodology and legal 

geography offer conceptual frameworks for studying the dynamics that shape the EU enlargement 

process, the implications of the Copenhagen criteria, and post-accession conditionality mechanisms in 

terms of “centre–periphery formation” within the Union, thereby illuminating the trajectories of EU 

integration in a unique and dynamic manner. The centre–periphery model therefore serves as a tool to 

depict the configuration and reconfiguration of EU Member States in times of crisis. For this reason, the 

following sections define and examine three stages of crisis—namely the economic crisis, the refugee 

crisis, and the current value-based crisis. As discussed in detail in the final part of this contribution, States’ 

interpretation and approach to EU values under Article 2 TEU may determine their position within an 

ideal “centre” of the Union or at its peripheries, depending on their adherence to a common 

understanding of the values enshrined in that article and their effective implementation and promotion5.  

 

2. The Formation of Centres and Peripheries within the EU integration process 

The path towards the enlargement of the European Union and the EU integration process, together with 

the formation of an EU constitutional identity, differs from those typical of state building processes, as 

the geographical criterion of Europeanness set out in the Treaties as the legal requirement for admission 

«does not have clear boundaries, and its application fades into the geographical continuity that 

characterizes the Euro-Asiatic continent»6. In other terms, the interrelation between the European 

territory and the community is determined by the territorial scope of the Treaties (Article 52 of the TEU 

and Article 355 TFEU)7. Moreover, the process of regional demarcation in the EU is shaped by the 

conditions outlined in Article 49 TEU and the principles that candidate countries must respect to become 

a member of the EU (Article 6.1). The Copenhagen criteria affect the legal and political context of both 

the Member States and the candidate countries, since they all must respect the founding values enshrined 

in Article 2 TEU, among which the respect of human rights, democracy, and the rule of law. As we will

When the designation of new Member States and candidate countries as peripheral served as the 

foundation for enlargement, the concept of an EU “centre” arose, normatively shaped by the intersection 

of political, economic, and legal standards, and the sharing of the same values promoted at the 

supranational level. Since the 1990s, the EU’s prospective enlargement to the former Communist 

countries in Central and Eastern Europe has raised questions about the institutional and policy reforms 

within the Union. Moreover, the resort to differentiated integration, which enables selected Member 

States to benefit from transitional arrangements, permanent derogations, and enhanced cooperation 

mechanisms, has shaped the political, social, and legal configuration of the EU over time. Especially in 

times of crisis and political dissent, politicians and academics discuss different approaches to integration: 

“multi-speed Europe” 8, “a core federal Europe”9, “concentric circles” and other related terminology to 

call upon some EU Member States to derogate from selective policies and EU law provisions (under the 

framework of the TEU and the TFEU) or, on the contrary, to move on to advanced integration10.  

 Whilst not popular in the first stages of the EU enlargement, differentiation mechanisms have been 

crafted since the very beginning, and they have driven the path of EU integration. The number of treaty-

based differentiations increased over time and, with the accession of Bulgaria and Romania in 2007, 

differentiations attained an unprecedented level11. Differentiated integration in the EU is mostly “multi-

speed” differentiation, that is, it entails temporary derogations and differentiation limited by time, mainly 

in response to economic disparities among the Member States12. In this case, also the process of centre-

periphery formation is temporary, as differentiation between Member States is supposed to end after a

At the macro-level, the combination of these two types of differentiation – multispeed and multitier – 

created a “centre-periphery” configuration: six founding Member States, the Southern and the Eastern 

Eurozone Member States, and a periphery comprising the non-Eurozone Member States in Northern 

and Eastern Europe. Furthermore, as suggested by the studies of Schimmelfennig and Winzen, 

conducted by “mapping and measuring” the development of multi-tier differentiation in EU accession 

treaties and legislation, a distinction can be drawn between the “core Europe”, i.e. a group of States that 

are uniformly integrated (with only a few treaty-based opt-outs or exemptions mechanisms)15, and 

different groups of peripheral countries. The EU differentiation increases as we move from the core 

toward the peripheral circles of States16. This is particularly evident in the shaping of the Eurozone, since 

States that are entirely out of the common currency system, but show a low level of differentiation in 

other policy domains, may be considered as part of a “semi-periphery”. The same discourse applies to 

the Schengen area. This configuration suggests that the EU «multi-tier integration is not dominated by a 

small vanguard of highly integrated core countries but produced by a small group of laggards and 

refusers»17.

in legislation in Estonia, Greece, Latvia, and Slovakia has reduced over time20. Northern countries as

Denmark, Sweden, Ireland, and the UK (until Brexit occurred), instead, greatly contributed to

Moreover, EU States can decide to pursue deeper integration through the implementation of enhanced 

cooperation mechanisms under Article 20 TEU and Articles 326-334 TFEU within the framework of the 

Union’s non-exclusive competences22. Different configurations of “cores and peripheries” may emerge, 

according to the political and legal domain under consideration. In this respect, it is worth noting that 

flexible integration under enhanced cooperation cannot exceed the boundaries of competences that all 

founding States have agreed upon. As is known, this is only possible by amending the Treaties in 

accordance with Article 48 TEU, a procedure that requires the consent of all EU members. These 

provisions function as a fundamental lock to prevent the emergence of “sub-Unions”, or “sub-cores”, 

reaching a new level of integration on matters that should be discussed by all Member States; thus, 

endangering the stability of the EU and eroding its founding principles and core legal basis23.

very beginning of European integration, economists have pointed out that the EU Member States’ 

economic structures differ in terms of the strength of the welfare state, GDP per capita, nature of 

flexicurity, and fiscal and economic policies25, among other factors.  

Whilst the studies on the socio-economic heterogeneity in the EU have always been part of the European 

integration literature26, core-periphery approaches have mostly concentrated on economic convergence 

and integration. With the outbreak of the sovereign debt crisis and the crisis of the Eurozone, first, and 

the COVID-19 pandemic, later, new studies on core-periphery relations in terms of divergence and 

cleavages between Member States came to the fore.  

As claimed by scholars in economics, the EU is not a uniform economy, but rather a dualistic one27. 

According to this view, one can distinguish between a “core” and a peripheral economic Europe-or, more 

accurately, multiple European peripheries28. On this premise, some scholars identify the highly developed 

economies of Western and Northern Europe – Denmark, Sweden, Finland, the Netherlands, Belgium, 

and Luxembourg, Germany, France, Austria and, until Brexit, the UK – as the “core” or the “centre” of 

the EU, while the less stable economies of Southern Europe – Greece, Malta, Cyprus, Portugal, Spain – 

are considered the “periphery”. According to this grouping, the “central zone” of the Union encompasses 

Hungary, the Czech Republic, Slovenia, Slovakia, and Poland, while the Eastern periphery is composed 

of Bulgaria, Romania, Croatia, Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania29. Three States are located at the boundary 

between the “core” and the “periphery”: Ireland, due to its economic dynamism and investment in 

research and development; France, owing to its stagnating economy; and Italy, because of the economic 

imbalances between the North and the South30.  

Against this background, the EU’s legal framework and policymaking raise pertinent questions 

concerning the distribution of resources (for instance, investment in energy and renewable resources, 

institutional development, and political access), costs (such as realignment of domestic institutions, 

political economies, greening mandates, migration, refugees and border management), and priorities

among EU countries31. In this respect, comprehending the core-periphery tension in the articulation of 

political strategies, geo-political action, and internal reforms is crucial for the EU, as it can either stabilize 

or disrupt the European polity formation, integration, and enlargement processes32. Moreover, the 

conflicts arising from differing perceptions of the European Union by European citizens generate 

tensions between core and peripheral countries in the Union, affecting the European Union as a whole.  

This was particularly evident during the financial and sovereign debt crisis, and in the management of the 

economic consequences of COVID-19. The economic crisis brought a change of paradigm in the EU. 

From the acknowledgment of different levels of economic development and integration between 

Member States, the political economy literature on European integration started depicting the divide 

between Northern “saintly creditors” and Southern “sinful debtors”33. Relying on this narrative, EU 

policymaking addressed the crisis through the “moral hazard paradigm”, assuming the sole responsibility 

of each national government for the state of their own financial conditions and policies34. Following this 

assumption, the response was based on adopting exemplary “virtuous” models35. There was a consensus 

on locating Germany at the “core” of the group of countries in the Eurozone, while Greece, Italy, 

Portugal, and Spain were conventionally seen as forming the “inner periphery”. Other EU Member States 

outside the Eurozone were also located in the EU periphery. Moreover, countries of the “outer 

periphery”, such as Bulgaria and Romania, were just as much affected by the Eurozone crisis as the “inner 

periphery”, even though they did not join the monetary union, due to their economic relations with the 

Eurozone in terms of trade, investment, labour market migration, integration of the financial sector.  

The North–South divide that emerged following the economic crisis was particularly evident, whereby 

both creditor and debtor states somehow felt short-changed by the measures taken by the Member States 

and the EU to confront the crisis36. The narrative of exemplar models to be followed by Southern 

peripheral countries guided the economic policies of the Eurozone, also fostering the emergence of 

Eurosceptic and nationalistic parties, such as, for instance, Syriza in Greece, at the national and European 

elections37.

When the COVID-19 pandemic hit in early 2020, the North–South divide discourse seemed to play itself 

out again. However, building on the experience of the economic and financial crisis, the capacity of 

managing the crisis was acknowledged as also being the result of structural imbalances between the EU 

centre and economic peripheral countries38. Despite some northern States, such as Denmark, being 

initially sceptical in setting aside a conditionality approach to the crisis39, the EU Member States managed 

to agree on a redistributive recovery fund, known as NextGenerationEU40. Italy and other Member States, 

which were considered to remain at the periphery of the EU during the economic and financial crisis, 

became gravely affected by the pandemic and thus considered it a litmus test for the Union and its 

capacity to act in financial solidarity in the face of emergencies, as well as in the common economic 

interest.

between Member States41. It is not in the scope of this essay to address the said principle and mechanisms 

in EU law in detail. Suffice it to say that the Treaties expressly mention the importance of solidarity 

(Articles 2 and 3 of the TEU)42 as a founding principle of the EU. The concept remains rather undefined

as a principle of EU primary law43, covering solidarity among European peoples44, intergenerational 

solidarity (Article 3.3 TEU), solidarity among Member states (Article 3(5) TEU), solidarity in the context 

of EU external action (Article 21 TEU) and other policy areas45. Despite its breadth, as a central value of 

the EU integration, solidarity has permeated EU law and policymaking since the beginning of the 

European project. However, the financial dimension of the principle did not emerge in ECJ case law46 

and scholarly literature until the outbreak of the EU economic crisis and the COVID-19 emergency, 

guiding the measures adopted by the EU and the Member States to confront them47. 

At present, financial assistance constitutes the predominant solidarity mechanism in the EU. However, 

as observed in scholarship, «the predominant facet of solidarity [among the Member States] is necessity. 

On the other hand, if one identifies solidarity with mutual trust, it can be seen to be currently lacking. 

The lack of mutual trust among EU Member States, as well as between Member States and EU 

institutions, is the common denominator of a number of problems encountered by the EU today»48. 

Ultimately, solidarity coincides with the national border, as it only stems from interests, political reality, 

and economic necessities of each Member State. As we will see in the following section, the lack of 

mutual trust and the sole reliance on “necessity” and financial burden sharing do little to promote EU 

integration, deepening cleavages and divergences between EU countries. The limits of a nation-based, 

rather than EU-based, supranational concept of solidarity are particularly evident when addressing 

another major EU crisis: the migration and asylum crisis.

guided by necessity and political opportunity. However, some scholars argue that the management of the

would have required a genuine supranational (federal) approach to solidarity49. In this respect, a “spatial 

reading of the law”50 through the lens of the core-periphery approach and comparative law methodologies 

can foster the comprehension of the effect of EU migration and refugee law on the re-shaping of human 

geography in Europe, as well as the recent re-configuration of the legal framework governing migrants 

and asylum seekers in the EU. Moreover, the analysis of cleavages and convergences among EU Member 

States in terms of migration and asylum policies sheds light on the progress of EU integration.  

Although usually associated with emergency situations, migration pressure at the external borders of 

Europe, as well as the movement of people between EU Member States, is a permanent and structural 

issue. The management of the so-called economic migrants, asylum seekers, and refugees, the 

externalization of responsibility to the other Member States within the EU or to the non-EU 

neighbouring states, the notions of safe and first asylum country, financial aid and administrative support 

constantly shape the spatial dimension of migration and asylum policies in the EU.  

With specific regard to asylum seekers, in the accession process, particularly in the 2004 and 2007 

enlargement, «EU membership was offered in exchange for transposing international obligations through 

the EU asylum acquis, collectivizing external border control, and shifting refugee ‘responsibility’ to new 

Member States with minimal standards for refugee protection and weak enforcement mechanisms»51. 

Starting from 2000, all States in Southern, Central, and Northern Europe introduced or amended laws 

and policies regarding asylum seekers and refugees to cope with the increasing migratory pressure coming 

from the Southern route and Eastern routes. The implemented policies considerably varied depending 

on geographical and political factors.  

During the 2015-2016 migration and refugee crisis, Southern and South-Eastern countries at the 

periphery of the EU and the Schengen area, faced with increasing pressure of migrants and asylum seekers 

at their borders, disregarded their obligations under the Common European Asylum System (CEAS) and 

became corridors to the North and Western European states. Consequently, the Schengen system was de 

facto suspended, and most countries decided to impose border control: for instance, Austria, Sweden, 

Norway, France, and Germany. The latter also declared that it would accept all the Syrian refugees 

entering its territory52; thus, disregarding the procedures established by the Dublin III regulation, which

of the EU, calling for the management of the humanitarian, socio-economic, and political consequences

and from extra-European countries, is changing the composition of the receiving countries. This may 

affect positively and negatively, depending on the countries considered, the competition for jobs and 

welfare, creating new cleavages between EU Member States or exacerbating existing ones, with 

consequences in terms of economic inequality and distributive effects within the Union56. 

In terms of core-periphery relations and cleavages, the “refugee crisis” mostly followed the path of the 

economic and Eurozone crisis, with political and economic divides among EU Member States stifling

a supranational, European approach to migration and refugee policy. Recently, the solidarity principle 

has been at the basis of the New EU Pact on Migration and Asylum, which will apply in 2026, aimed at 

harmonizing migration and refugee law in the EU and introducing a common framework for 

responsibility sharing58.  

As is known, the Schengen and Dublin 1990 conventions placed the burden of border controls and 

asylum applications upon the Member States located at the external borders of the EU. Due to extremely 

limited results of the relocation on a voluntary basis during the 2015 refugee crisis, the EU Member States 

agreed on mandatory solidarity. However, to accommodate the concerns of Eastern Member States, and 

particularly the Visegrad group, the solidarity mechanism has been based on flexibility, so that EU 

countries can choose among three types of solidarity: relocation, financial assistance, and alternative 

measures. However, relocation takes place only in cases of rescue operations at sea or in cases of severe 

pressure at the border due to a migratory crisis declared by the Commission. In other words, solidarity 

through relocation remains an emergency solution. Moreover, the Pact does not establish any 

enforcement mechanism to ensure the implementation of the solidarity mechanisms59. Member States at 

the external borders will only be partially relieved of the burden carried by the influx of people. Moreover, 

the solidarity would remain focused on the economic dimension, providing for the intervention of 

Member States to take care of people from an administrative point of view as well as assistance, only in 

emergency situations; in short, solidarity would continue to be placed in an emergency dimension. Some 

countries, such as Lithuania, announced that they would prefer to give financial assistance rather than 

relocate, while the Visegrad group continues to oppose the pact. Overall, whilst the word “solidarity” is 

mentioned 225 times in the Pact, it is likely to reproduce (and institutionalise) the core-periphery 

dynamics that emerged during the 2015-2016 crisis.  

The intergovernmental roots of migration policy in the EU and the resort to differentiated governance 

make migration policy harmonization and solidarity among EU members States particularly complex to 

achieve. While mostly addressed separately in literature, the migration and refugee crises can be 

considered two of the many “faces” of the “rule of law backsliding” in some of the countries in the EU. 

Moreover, together with the Covid-19 emergency, they created new cleavages between the Member States 

regarding the values on which these policies are based or reinforced the already existing ones.

The rule of law crisis in border management emerged, for instance, in the arbitrary application of legal 

concepts, such as safe country of origin, first country of arrival, and safe third country; the lack of 

certainty in the interpretation and the implementation gap of the provisions regulating the CEAS clashes 

with the functioning of the rule of law60. The same goes for the systemic violation of fundamental human 

rights of migrants, asylum seekers, and refugees at the outbreak of the crisis in 201561.  

 

5. The Value-based Cleavage  

As was previously explained, alongside contingent factors, such as geographic positions, economic 

conditions and the political landscape, the response of some Central and Eastern countries to the refugee 

crisis and the subsequent reform of the CEAS can also be read as resulting from a disagreement over the 

interpretation of the values enshrined in Article 2 TEU that should underpin the European Union. This 

interpretation is strictly dependent on the legal-historical background of the post-communist countries 

which influences their approach towards the rule of law and fundamental human rights62.  

It is not by chance that the value-based cleavage analysed in this paragraph emerged after the enlargement 

of 2004 and gave rise to a new debate on what represents the unamendable identity of the EU as a 

constitutional project (also new vis-à-vis the approach adopted in the Opinions 1/91 and 2/13 of the 

ECJ). It is true that this discussion on values had opened up in the early 2000s with respect to another 

“non-post-communist” Member State, i.e. Austria, that had accessed the EU in 1995. Rule of law 

concerns started then, but the current challenges and responses have a distinct scope and significantly 

greater relevance. In the well-known 2022 “conditionality case”, the values in Article 2 TEU were defined 

by the ECJ as the «very identity of the European Union»63, with an expression that, until that moment, 

had mainly been used to protect the Member States’ identity vis-à-vis integration. In fact, so far, the 

identity factor had taken on various forms depending on the area in which it was applied and, above all, 

on the side which used it to defend its interests. Identity can belong to a single Member State, in which 

case we would be dealing with the concept of national identity; it can be predicated on a set of States that 

share certain similarities, in which case we would find ourselves within the syncretic aspect of the identity 

factor, materialized through common constitutional traditions; but we have also found, as a result of

recent jurisprudential developments, this creation of a “reactive” identity, specific to the European

The values of Article 2 TEU are at the core of the EU integration project65 and over the past decade have 

been provided with a series of instruments to be defended, especially as the value of the rule of law is 

concerned. As is well known, the main tools to address the “rule of law backsliding” are ECJ cases on 

judicial independence, infringement procedures, the elaboration of the principle of non-regression, the 

conditionality regulation, and yearly Rule of Law reports by the Commission. 

The ECJ has often been called upon to act and has been particularly effective in uncovering systemic 

violations of the rule of law by Member States. In the seminal judgments on conditionality,66 the ECJ 

dismissed the argument put forward by Poland and Hungary, who claimed that within the framework of 

budgetary obligations the concept of the rule of law was too vague. The ECJ, instead, claimed that the 

rule of law is part of the European countries’ constitutional traditions so that the States can delimit and 

“concretise” the content of that principle.  

So far, the ECJ case law shows that the values enshrined in Article 2 have been applied alongside specific 

provisions of EU law, particularly Article.19 TEU and Article 47 CFR, serving the purposes of “mutual 

amplification”67. In the Case C-769/22 against Hungary, instead, the Commission relied on Article 2 

TEU as a freestanding ground for action for the first time. The analysis of the challenges and 

opportunities raised by recognizing Article 2 TEU as an autonomous ground for action exceeds the 

purpose of this essay and has been covered by the literature in recent times with respect to the pending 

case against Hungary68 and the response to the threats posed by the Anti-LGBTQ+ law and, above all, 

the possibility of invoking Article 2 to tackle the alleged violations of the EU rule of law. 

 Some States criticised this choice by arguing that relying on Article 2 TEU as a self-standing provision, 

without a specific European domain being involved, would exceed the scope of EU law and of the 

competences of the Union69. However, in spite of these points remaining controversial, the Commission’s

plea was supported by 16 Member States, together with the European Parliament, and civil society 

organizations, affirming that the Hungarian legislation affects the very essence of European society70.  

In this respect, the emerging concept of European society may serve as a basis for a common 

understanding of the values protected in Article 2 TEU and, eventually, affect the cleavages between the 

EU’s centre and the peripheries, shifting them from a conflictual relationship to a dialectical interaction. 

The case law of domestic constitutional courts could serve as «a powerful tool to bridge the gap between 

domestic constitutional identities and the identity of the EU, to emphasize axiological similarities and 

convergence»71. While in the case at hand, it is not obvious that the ECJ will use Article 2 TEU alone (in 

spite of the Advocate General’s Opinion72), the forthcoming Hungarian judgment will provide the Court 

and the legal doctrine with a concrete occasion to discuss the justiciability and direct effect of the EU 

values, the challenges of such a choice by the ECJ, and its possible implications in terms of European 

integration.  

This is particularly important due to the current rule of law crisis and considering the (existing or 

perceived) cleavages between the “core” and the “periphery” of the Union regarding the interpretation 

of the meaning and the scope of the EU values listed in Article 2 TEU, as well as the disagreement among 

EU countries concerning the enforcement of the respect of the rule of law. In this respect, some raised 

the concern that defining those values could potentially be detrimental to constitutional autonomy and 

contrast with constitutional identities of the Member States within the framework of the integration 

process73. On the other hand, an “European rule of law” has already been defined in the Rule of Law 

Conditionality Regulation 2020/2092, which has reinforced the autonomy-related concerns about the 

meaning of those values and about the interaction between Member States’ systems and EU law. 

 

6. To Conclude: Towards Even More Swinging Cleavages? 

The discussion on the Union’s values relates to the ongoing effort to build a European constitutional 

identity, completing the transition from an economic community to a community of shared values74. The 

constitutional transformation of the EU is reflected also in the transformation of the yardsticks or 

conflicts that define the centre and the periphery of the Union.

The values shaping the constitutional identity of the Union are in a dynamic relationship with the 

common constitutional traditions of the Member States, their constitutional identities, and their national 

identities75. However, while the Eastern enlargement has been generally considered a success76, 

comparativists are aware of the difficulties of bringing closer countries with different political and legal 

traditions77. It is true that, at the beginning, the EU witnessed a gradual convergence among the new 

Member States, despite their differences in history, culture, and legal traditions. However, the idea of 

“convergence” implies a path from the “periphery” to the “centre”: a process of joining the “EU club” 

through progressive efforts to bridge the gaps and cleavages78. One might ask whether this process is 

one-way, or whether it entails mutual learning. The new Member States may also contribute to shaping 

European identity through their own political and legal heritage, including specific interpretations of 

Article 2 TEU79.  

Again, the approach of countries such as Hungary, Poland, Slovakia, and the Czech Republic to the 

management of the migrant and refugee crisis is emblematic of the policy divergences characterizing the 

EU and different approaches to the rule of law due to geographical, historical, and political differences 

within the European countries. Indeed, before 2015-2016, the Central European States strongly asserted 

their status as part of the core of the EU (and, more broadly, their entry into the West). They had had to 

amend and adapt their legal and political system to conform to the acquis communautaire, and they shaped 

their narrative to affirm their Europeanness80. However, when confronted with the massive migratory 

pressure, those countries (as others located in the Southern periphery of the EU) distanced themselves 

from the centre of the Union, which called for the implementation of solidarity and burden sharing 

principle enshrined in EU law, advocated in particular by Germany. As affirmed by Tabosa, «[t]he 

migration crisis, together with the illiberal turn happening in Poland and Hungary, led to the diffusion of 

a strongly securitized discourse in the Visegrad countries that portrays migration»81. To reject the quota 

mechanisms envisaged by the Commission to reduce pressure on Southern (peripheral) countries,

fledged constitutional community. This evolution suggests that the EU’s centre–periphery dynamics will 

continue to oscillate, reflecting both unity and divergence within its constitutional order.

Il diritto a conoscere le proprie origini tra 

adozione e fecondazione eterologa,  

alla luce della giurisprudenza costituzionale* 

 

di Alberto Randazzo 

Professore associato di Diritto costituzionale e pubblico 

Università degli Studi di Messina  

 

Abstract [It]: Lo scritto indaga la tutela accordata, alla luce della giurisprudenza costituzionale, al diritto a 
conoscere le proprie origini (il cui rilievo, anche rispetto al diritto all’identità personale, viene messo in risalto) in 
caso di adozione. L’A. mira a verificare – anche in prospettiva de jure condendo – in che misura tale diritto possa 
essere riconosciuto e garantito nell’ipotesi di fecondazione eterologa, da tempo ammessa nel nostro ordinamento.   
 
Title: The Italian constitutional case law in the matter of the right of those who have been adopted or born through 
heterologous fertilization to know their own origins 
Abstract [En]: The essay focuses on the constitutional case law in the matter of the right of those who have been 
adopted. Such a right assumes an importance even in relation to the safeguard of the right of personal identity. 
Moreover, the paper aims to verify – in a de jure condendo perspective – how such a right could be recognized and 
guaranteed in case of heterologous fertilization. Indeed, for many years Italian citizens have been allowed to access 
this practice. 
 
Parole-chiave: origini, adozione, fecondazione eterologa, Costituzione, giurisprudenza costituzionale 
Keywords: origins, adoption, heterologous fertilization, Constitution, case law 
 
Sommario: 1. Premessa. 2. L’importanza di conoscere le proprie origini e le ricadute sull’esistenza umana. Cenni. 
3. Il diritto del figlio adottivo a conoscere le proprie origini. 4. (Segue) Un passo indietro nella giurisprudenza 
costituzionale e altre considerazioni. 5. Riflettendo sulla giurisprudenza costituzionale richiamata. 6. Il diritto a 
conoscere le proprie origini e la fecondazione eterologa. 7. La conoscenza delle proprie origini tra etica e diritto: 
brevi considerazioni conclusive.

conoscere le proprie origini significhi poter risalire ai dati anagrafici dei genitori ossia alla c.d. “piena

naturale ha scelto (nel caso di adozione) di mantenere ignota la propria identità (il c.d. “parto anonimo”)96,

Tuttavia, sono ipotizzabili circostanze in cui un soggetto sia all’oscuro della propria storia parentale per

Infine, occorre distinguere il caso in cui il diritto a conoscere le proprie origini abbia alla base ragioni 

connesse al bene-salute da quello che abbia origine nel desiderio di veder pienamente realizzata la propria 

identità. In questa sede, com’è chiaro, si sta facendo riferimento a questa seconda ipotesi100. 

 

2. L’importanza di conoscere le proprie origini e le ricadute sull’esistenza umana. Cenni 

La conoscenza delle proprie origini e, quindi, della propria storia personale e “parentale” appaiono 

strettamente collegate, in quanto funzionali, all’esercizio di una serie diritti – sui quali non ci si soffermerà 

specificamente – che sono stati riconosciuti, nel tempo, dalla Corte costituzionale, il che rende la suddetta 

conoscenza meritevole di particolare garanzia. In particolare, si fa riferimento al diritto all’identità 

personale101 (che, com’è stato detto, rappresenta la «sintesi della storia di ciascun individuo»)102, ma anche 

ai connessi diritti al nome103 e alla reputazione, al diritto al rispetto dei dati personali, al diritto alla salute

È noto che l’identità personale è data da una serie di fattori, sui quali non è possibile in questa sede 

intrattenersi; tra questi ultimi sono da annoverare, oltre al nome120, le caratteristiche fisiche e 

comportamentali, le attitudini, gli interessi personali, la professione, le relazioni sociali che intesse un 

soggetto durante la propria vita, ma anche il patrimonio genetico che si eredita dai genitori121. 

A sostegno di quanto si sta dicendo, soccorre la giurisprudenza (costituzionale e di merito).  

Ad esempio, con la sent. n. 286 del 2016, il giudice delle leggi, oltre a ribadire la «copertura costituzionale 

assoluta» del diritto all’identità personale nell’art. 2 della Carta, ha rilevato l’importanza del nome e ha 

messo in luce il «paritario rilievo di entrambe le figure genitoriali nel processo di costruzione di tale 

identità personale»122. 

Anche la Corte di cassazione, in più occasioni, ha avuto modo di esprimersi sul punto. Si pensi, tra le 

altre, alla sent. n. 6963 del 2018123, della sez. I civ. Vale la pena riportare testualmente quanto ha affermato 

la Corte suprema, per la quale «il diritto a conoscere le proprie origini costituisce un’espressione essenziale 

del diritto all’identità personale. Lo sviluppo equilibrato della personalità individuale e relazionale si 

realizza soprattutto attraverso la costruzione della propria identità esteriore, di cui il nome e la 

discendenza giuridicamente rilevante e riconoscibile costituiscono elementi essenziali, e di quella 

interiore». Sembra molto interessante rilevare questa distinzione operata dalla Cassazione tra identità 

“esteriore” e identità “interiore”, che comporrebbero l’identità personale nella sua complessità ed 

interezza. È alla seconda che il giudice della legittimità riconduce l’importanza, per ogni individuo, di 

conoscere le proprie origini. Una compita ricostruzione della propria identità, infatti, «può richiedere la 

conoscenza e l’accettazione della discendenza biologica e della rete parentale più prossima». Tuttavia,

l’aspetto relazionale che costituisce l’identità personale rileva anche in riferimento al rapporto che si 

instaura tra «chi dà origine e chi prende origine». Infatti, non è mancato chi ha osservato che «l’idea del 

nascere da qualcuno non può essere assorbita, mediante una finzione, nell’idea di essere educati da 

qualcuno»124.  

In ogni caso, si è dell’avviso che l’identità personale sia un “tutto” indistinguibile, nella quale corpo e 

anima si fondono sicché la “componente” esteriore ha implicazioni su quella interiore e viceversa. 

Pertanto, se un essere umano desidera conoscere se stesso, operazione per nulla agevole e che 

probabilmente dura tutta la vita125, occorre anche conoscere chi lo ha procreato. D’altra parte, se si ha il 

diritto a vivere e ad avere una propria identità si deve poter godere anche del diritto a conoscersi, ma 

perché quest’ultimo venga davvero garantito occorre – necessariamente – conoscere chi sono i propri 

genitori. 

Si potrebbe ulteriormente osservare che per comprendere “chi siamo” dobbiamo sapere “da dove 

veniamo”126. Così potremmo anche comprendere “dove possiamo andare”. A quest’ultimo proposito, 

infatti, non si può negare che la possibilità di progettare il futuro, di porsi degli obiettivi (auspicabilmente 

raggiungibili), ma anche di nutrire speranze ha molto a che fare con la conoscenza di se stessi, delle 

proprie capacità e qualità ma anche dei propri limiti e fragilità. 

Per le ragioni alle quali si sta qui facendo cenno (e per molte altre alle quali non è ora possibile accennare), 

ben si comprende il rilievo che ha la conoscenza delle proprie origini, tanto da poter considerare 

quest’ultima oggetto di un diritto pubblico soggettivo (e quindi di una pretesa che si può far valere nei 

confronti dell’ordinamento)127 che però, come accennato e come adesso si dirà meglio, non ha trovato 

fino ad oggi piena tutela. Infine, occorre chiedersi se il diritto a conoscere le proprie origini sia 

configurabile come diritto “autonomo” o se debba essere considerato solo alla luce del rapporto di 

strumentalità che lo lega, come detto, ad altri diritti.    

 

3. Il diritto del figlio adottivo a conoscere le proprie origini  

Come si diceva poco sopra, il diritto che si sta qui discutendo si è affermato in materia di adozione, 

trovando al riguardo una forma (seppur limitata, come si dirà) di garanzia. È, infatti, in questo ambito

Nella terza delle decisioni della Cassazione ora richiamate si legge, tra l’altro, che «il diritto dell’adottato a conoscere le 
proprie origini deve essere garantito anche nel caso in cui non sia più possibile procedere all’interpello della madre 
naturale», nel caso di specie, perché morta (p.to 5.3 della motivazione). Infatti, come osserva ancora la Cassazione, 
«l’interpretazione della norma che identifichi nell’intervenuta morte della donna, un ostacolo assoluto al riconoscimento 
del diritto a conoscere le proprie origini da parte dell’adottato, determinerebbe un’ingiustificata disparità di trattamento 
tra i figli nati da donne che hanno scelto l’anonimato ma non sono più in vita e i figli di donne che possono essere 
interpellate sulla reversibilità della scelta operata alla nascita. Tale opzione ermeneutica sarebbe, inoltre, viziata di 
irragionevolezza perché sottoporrebbe il riconoscimento e l’esercizio di un diritto della persona di primario rilievo ad 
un fattore meramente eventuale quale quello del momento in cui si chiede il riconoscimento del proprio diritto» (p.to 
5.3 della motivazione). 
Pertanto, effettuando un ragionevole bilanciamento degli interessi in campo, si può osservare che la morte della madre 
biologica non possa pregiudicare il diritto a conoscere le proprie origini e che non rilevi il mancato decorso del termine 
dei cento anni, tuttavia, l’esercizio di tale diritto non può ledere quello di terzi interessati. Nello specifico, il dispositivo 
della sent. n. 22838 recita: «il diritto dell’adottato – nato da donna che abbia dichiarato alla nascita di non voler essere 
nominata D.P.R. n. 396 del 2000, ex art. 30, comma 1 – ad accedere alle informazioni concernenti la propria origine e 
l’identità della madre biologica sussiste e può essere concretamente esercitato anche se la stessa sia morta e non sia 
possibile procedere alla verifica della perdurante attualità della scelta di conservare il segreto, non rilevando nella 
fattispecie il mancato decorso del termine di cento anni dalla formazione del certificato di assistenza al parto o della 
cartella clinica di cui al D.Lgs. n. 196 del 2003, art. 93, commi 2 e 3, salvo il trattamento lecito e non lesivo dei diritti di 
terzi dei dati personali conosciuti». 
A quest’ultimo proposito, la tutela dei diritti degli eredi della donna che aveva optato per l’anonimato non può che essere 
recessiva dinanzi alla richiesta di accertamento avanzata dal figlio (così, quasi testualmente, si legge al p.to 4 della 
motivazione della dec. del 2022 che, al riguardo, richiama le sentenze del 2016 sopra richiamate). Inoltre, nella dec. n. 
26616 si afferma: «Solo la madre vivente può manifestare il proprio dissenso alla richiesta del figlio, nell’esercizio di 
propri personalissimi diritti soggettivi; in caso di decesso, invece, il figlio può essere autorizzato dal Tribunale minorile 
ad accedere alle informazioni riservate sull’identità della propria madre, senza particolari ostacoli» (p.to 4 della 
motivazione). 
In dottrina, cfr. V. DE SANTIS, Diritto a conoscere le proprie origini…, cit., p. 14; R. TREZZA, Diritto all’anonimato e diritto a 
conoscere le proprie origini biologiche: un approccio “sbilanciatorio-avaloriale” o “bilanciatorio-assiologico”?, in Giustizia insieme, 4 ottobre

144 Sembra opportuno ricordare il ragionamento fatto nella sentenza in parola del 25 settembre 2012. La Corte EDU,

e in particolare le circostanze di quest’ultima, rientra nella vita privata del bambino, e poi dell’adulto, sancita dall’articolo

mantenere l’anonimato, viene inevitabilmente data una preferenza incondizionata a questi ultimi». Infatti, «se la madre 
biologica ha deciso di mantenere l’anonimato, la normativa italiana non dà alcuna possibilità al figlio adottivo e non 
riconosciuto alla nascita di chiedere l’accesso ad informazioni non identificative sulle sue origini o la reversibilità del 
segreto. In queste condizioni, la Corte ritiene che l’Italia non abbia cercato di stabilire un equilibrio e una proporzionalità 
tra gli interessi delle parti in causa e abbia dunque oltrepassato il margine di discrezionalità che le è stato accordato». Alla 
luce di quanto detto, la Corte EDU rileva la violazione dell’art. 8 CEDU. 
È da notare, alla luce della sent. Godelli c. Italia, che i limiti posti al figlio (ormai adulto) di conoscere le proprie origini è 
solo riferito alla madre; il padre, che non ha riconosciuto un figlio, sarebbe sempre possibile ricercarlo (cfr. P. PAZÉ, 
Le informazioni delle origini ai genitori adottivi, in Minorigiustizia, n. 2, 2013, p. 201).   
145 Al riguardo, si può osservare che, sebbene non ci sia dubbio che la Consulta sia stata condizionata dalla Corte di 
Strasburgo, l’illegittimità costituzionale pronunciata nella sent. n. 278 del 2013 non si fonda sulla violazione dell’art. 117, 
I comma, Cost. Infatti, il ragionamento operato dal giudice delle leggi porta quest’ultimo a tutelare l’adottato, scalfendo 
il muro issato dal legislatore nella l. n. 184 del 1983, come modificato dal d.lgs. n. 196 del 2003, in modo “autonomo”, 
quasi a voler dire che – senza la necessità di invocare l’art. 8 CEDU ritenuto violato nel caso Godelli c. Italia – 
l’ordinamento (e, specificamente, la Costituzione) contenga gli “strumenti” idonei a garantire quell’“equilibrio” e quella 
“proporzionalità” dei quali discorreva la Corte EDU tra il diritto all’anonimato e il diritto a conoscere le proprie origini.  
Sul punto, v. E. FRONTONI, Il diritto del figlio a conoscere le proprie origini…, cit., pp. 6 ss. In questo senso, l’A. rileva che 
«la giurisprudenza sovranazionale costituisce per il giudice costituzionale lo stimolo, ma anche l’occasio per una rilettura 
delle norme costituzionali» in materia. Pertanto, il merito che ha avuto la sent. Godelli c. Italia è stato, come osserva 
Frontoni, quello di avere «innesca[to] un circolo virtuoso», essendo stato uno «stimolo per il giudice a risollevare la 
questione di legittimità» nonostante l’infondatezza pronunciata dalla Corte costituzionale nella precedente dec. n. 425 
del 2005.

genitori adottivi vengano date maggiori informazioni sui genitori naturali.  

Con molta probabilità, la motivazione che sta alla base di tale “squilibrio” è quella valutata anche nella 

precedente sent. n. 425 del 2005 della Corte; si voleva, per un verso, «che il parto avvenisse nelle 

condizioni ottimali tanto per la madre che per il figlio» e, per altro verso, si mirava a «distogliere la donna 

da decisioni irreparabili, per quest’ultimo ben più gravi»149. E l’irrevocabilità degli effetti di questa scelta 

si ispirava ad una logica di rafforzamento dei corrispondenti obiettivi ora richiamati. Si voleva, infatti, 

escludere che la decisione per l’anonimato potesse comportare, per la madre, «il rischio di essere, in un

per decidere se confermare o revocare quella lontana dichiarazione di volontà»150. In altre parole, il favore 

nei confronti dell’anonimato trovava la sua ragion d’essere nel diritto alla vita dello stesso nascituro.

La rigida normativa intaccata dal giudice delle leggi, in fin dei conti, entrava in conflitto sia con il diritto 

a conoscere la propria storia parentale da parte del nato da madre ignota sia con il diritto di quest’ultima 

di cambiare idea; in poche parole, ad essere lese erano sia la libertà di autodeterminazione dell’adottato 

che quella della madre. La volontà della donna veniva, infatti, “cristallizzata”, subendo una 

“immobilizzazione”; infatti, «una volta intervenuta la scelta per l’anonimato […], la relativa 

manifestazione di volontà assume[va] connotati di irreversibilità destinati, sostanzialmente, ad 

“espropriare” la persona titolare del diritto da qualsiasi ulteriore opzione»151. 

Come osservato dalla Corte nella sent. n. 278, quanto ora detto è stato «icasticamente scolpito dall’art. 

93, comma 2, del […] d.lgs. n. 196 del 2003, secondo cui “il certificato di assistenza al parto o la cartella 

clinica, ove comprensivi dei dati personali che rendono identificabile la madre che abbia dichiarato di non 

voler essere nominata avvalendosi della facoltà di cui all’articolo 30, comma 1, del decreto del Presidente 

della Repubblica 3 novembre 2000, n. 396, possono essere rilasciati in copia integrale a chi vi abbia 

interesse, in conformità alla legge, decorsi cento anni dalla formazione del documento”»152. 

Ben si comprende che il limite temporale dei cento anni vanificava e “ridicolizzava” il diritto a conoscere 

le proprie origini, che finiva per esistere solo in astratto in quanto sarebbe stato difficilmente esercitabile. 

Infatti, non solo era improbabile che un essere umano arrivasse a “tagliare” questo “traguardo”; ammesso 

pure che ciò avvenisse, quel soggetto avrebbe potuto, a quel punto, colmare un vuoto e “morire in pace”, 

ma conoscere le proprie origini, a quell’età, di certo a poco (o a nulla) sarebbe servito per “completare” 

la “costruzione” del proprio “io” anche in vista di prospettive future. 

Tali scelte legislative, però, erano fondate «sulla ritenuta esigenza di prevenire turbative nei confronti della 

madre in relazione all’esercizio di un suo “diritto all’oblio” e, nello stesso tempo, sull’esigenza di 

salvaguardare erga omnes la riservatezza circa l’identità della madre, evidentemente considerata come 

esposta a rischio ogni volta in cui se ne [potesse] cercare il contatto per verificare se intend[esse] o meno 

mantenere il proprio anonimato»; tuttavia, come la Corte ha osservato, «né l’una né l’altra esigenza 

[poteva] ritenersi dirimente»153. Come si legge nella sentenza, ieri come oggi, la rinuncia alla «genitorialità 

giuridica» non deve necessariamente comportare la rinuncia alla «genitorialità naturale», in quanto ciò 

ostacolerebbe un qualsiasi tipo di relazione tra madre e figlio con non poche ricadute sul piano della

Nel 2005, il V e il VI comma dell’art. 28 prevedevano, grazie alla modifica sopra citata del 2001, la 

possibilità per l’adottato di accedere, a certe condizioni, ai dati e alle informazioni che lo riguardavano. 

Tuttavia, la conoscenza delle proprie origini rimaneva molto limitata; infatti, al VII comma si impediva 

l’accesso alle informazioni qualora il minore non fosse riconosciuto alla nascita e anche uno solo dei 

genitori biologici avesse dichiarato di voler rimanere anonimo.  

Il d.lgs. n. 196 del 2003 è intervenuto sulla previsione da ultimo richiamata offrendo qualche possibilità 

in più all’adottato (v. art. 177, II comma)169; infatti, l’accesso alle informazioni relative alle proprie origini 

rimaneva inibito solo nel caso in cui fosse stata la madre a manifestare la volontà di rimanere anonima. 

Nella questione di legittimità costituzionale che ha condotto alla decisione n. 425 è stata proprio questa 

norma ad essere impugnata, apparendo comunque limitativa nei confronti del minore che avesse voluto 

ricostruire la propria storia parentale. 

In tale circostanza, il giudice delle leggi ha ritenuto non fondata la questione, non ravvisando la presunta 

violazione dell’art. 2 Cost., invocata dal rimettente. Come si legge nella sentenza, «la norma impugnata 

mira evidentemente a tutelare la gestante che – in situazioni particolarmente difficili dal punto di vista 

personale, economico o sociale – abbia deciso di non tenere con sé il bambino, offrendole la possibilità 

di partorire in una struttura sanitaria appropriata e di mantenere al contempo l’anonimato nella 

conseguente dichiarazione di nascita: e in tal modo intende – da un lato – assicurare che il parto avvenga 

in condizioni ottimali, sia per la madre che per il figlio, e – dall’altro – distogliere la donna da decisioni 

irreparabili, per quest’ultimo ben più gravi». La Corte costituzionale, pertanto, ha motivato così (ed ha 

espressamente dichiarato) l’assolutezza del diritto all’anonimato della madre, che non può essere in alcun 

modo limitato170. Sul punto, però, occorre soffermarsi un attimo, in quanto non si può fare a meno di

ed in potenziale conflitto tra loro” (sentenza n. 264 del 2012). Se così non fosse, si verificherebbe 

l’illimitata espansione di uno dei diritti, che diverrebbe “tiranno” nei confronti delle altre situazioni 

giuridiche costituzionalmente riconosciute e protette, che costituiscono, nel loro insieme, espressione 

della dignità della persona»172. 

Alla luce di quanto detto, il ragionamento operato dalla Consulta nel 2005 sarebbe condivisibile solo nella 

misura in cui si rilevasse che il diritto a conoscere le proprie origini non possa trovare fondamento in 

Costituzione a differenza del diritto all’anonimato della madre biologica, ma sarebbe da dimostrare la 

diversa “natura” dei due diritti; qualora si “facesse fatica” a riconoscere il fondamento costituzionale del 

diritto a conoscere le proprie origini, non meno agevole sarebbe riconoscere il suddetto fondamento al 

diritto all’anonimato. 

Tuttavia, sembra che le cose stiano diversamente, in quanto l’uno e l’altro diritto – come detto – appaiono 

impliciti nella Carta, trovando variamente ospitalità fra le trame di quest’ultima, in particolare agli artt. 2, 

3, 13 e 30, 117, I comma, Cost. Non manca chi riconduce il diritto a conoscere le proprie origini anche 

all’art. 21 Cost., sotto il profilo del «diritto di informazione e di accesso agli atti amministrativi»173.   

Non essendovi espressa menzione in Costituzione dei diritti ai quali si sta facendo riferimento, è possibile 

affermare che essi siano da annoverare – come accennato all’inizio – tra i cc.dd. “nuovi diritti”174, 

riconducibili al novero dei diritti inviolabili che la Repubblica è chiamata a riconoscere e a garantire ex 

art. 2 Cost., secondo l’idea – già sostenuta da chi scrive in precedenti occasioni – che quest’ultima 

previsione contenga una clausola “a fattispecie aperta”… ma non troppo175. 

Infatti, incidentalmente, si ricordi che il giudice delle leggi – con una sentenza che si potrebbe definire 

“storica”, la n. 98 del 1979 – ha osservato che «nella costante interpretazione della Corte, l’invocato art. 

2 della Costituzione, nel riconoscere i diritti inviolabili dell’uomo, che costituiscono patrimonio 

irretrattabile della sua personalità, deve essere ricollegato alle norme costituzionali concernenti singoli
